evidence_quotes.quote: shutdown of older releases (or restricting the access) of games and replacing them with new ones.
the shutdown of games like Overwatch and CS:GO were completely unessential and absolutely strap away customers' rights.
they discontinued official support for CS:GO
they never gave us, the customers, an option to download the old release separately from CS2
But in my opinion access to discontinued products shouldn't be so hard to access

paragraphs.text: I'd like to give some feedback regarding the Digital Fairness Act and more precisely the shutdown of older releases (or restricting the access) of games and replacing them with new ones. There is nothing wrong with making sequels to online service games, and that's absolutely fine, however the shutdown of games like Overwatch and CS:GO were completely unessential and absolutely strap away customers' rights. In my view, whenever I purchase anything I should have access to the thing I paid for, because as a customer I decided to buy the product, to support the product and to use the product according to the EULA of said product. As said before there isn't anything wrong with the industry to evolve and put their resources into something else, however restricting access or even shutting down their older products, without letting the customers to enjoy the very product they paid for. In my personal experience I have payed for CS:GO way back in 2018, I liked the game the way it is, it wasn't very intensive on my computer so I bought it. Up until 2023 there were countless of updates, that changed the game in numerous ways, but the product at its core was the same, just built upon. However when Valve released the CS2 title, they discontinued official support for CS:GO, which is within their rights and they didn't shutdown the game entirely, it was still downloadable. But they never gave us, the customers, an option to download the old release separately from CS2, they transferred all of their CS:GO reviews to their new game, which made their new release look better than it actually was. Therefore big part of these reviews are FALSE, because they were made for a whole different game! I never wanted to pay for CS2, I never wanted to play it, yet I am still forced to download this game if I want to play the old one. I am no saying they should continue their support, if they decide to focus their resources on their new project, then let it be. But in my opinion access to discontinued products shouldn't be so hard to access, and shouldn't force me to download their new product, just in order for me to get to the old one. And this is a lot better than what Blizzard has done, removing their whole old game in order to release a very similar one, which is free to play. They gave all original Overwatch players, some free microtransactions in Overwatch 2, but people didn't pay for the second game, they payed for the first one which after Overwatch 2's release was shut down, stripping access away from customers and without giving them a chance to experience the game they enjoyed. Some may argue that these are mere "updates" to the games, but why does these updates get announced as if they are whole new releases, why they rename their whole products? These new games can't be defined as updates, because they at the very least get announced as new releases. I'd love if developers provided an easy and fair access to older releases, even if they are discontinued, so we can consume the products we payed for. And whenever they decide to stop official support for a certain game, then at least companies should give the game private server support, so people can still try to run the game, without relying on financially unbeneficial support for and from the company.
